text:
Excellent — full 4-cardinal yaw overlap. Temporal comparison is well-grounded. Both years show:
- Aged medium-grey asphalt, oxidized
- Low-severity longitudinal cracking (sealed lane joints / minor)
- No alligator, potholes, patches, rutting
- No treatment signature visible in either epoch

The picture is clear: **stable mature asphalt, no treatment applied 2016→2025, distress remained low-severity longitudinal**. This is a clean **Fair** (mature asphalt with visible engineering distress, but well below Poor disqualifiers — no alligator, no potholes, no spalling). Actually, low-sev longitudinal cracks alone with no other distress is borderline Good/Fair. Per rubric: "0–4 short cracks per 5m, no rutting, no potholes, no patches failing → Good"; "≥5 cracks per 5m OR mottled patchwork OR edge fraying OR raveling → Fair".

The 2025 report says "multiple parallel hairline-to-narrow cracks" — that suggests density >4/5m. Combined with oxidized aged surface and no treatment in 9 years — this fits **Fair** (mature asphalt approaching mid-life, structural distress emerging).

input.rationale: Mature aged asphalt at east-end Sunset Blvd, surface generation appears unchanged 2016→2025 (9-year stable arc, full 4-cardinal yaw overlap between epochs). Both years show medium-grey oxidized asphalt with multiple parallel low-severity longitudinal cracks/sealed lane-joint seams in wheelpath direction. No alligator, potholes, patches, rutting, or spalling in either epoch. Critically: NO treatment signature (no overlay, slurry, or chip seal) between 2016 and 2025 — surface has aged in place ~10+ years untreated. Distress mix (longitudinal-only, low-severity, oxidation) is consistent with phase-1-to-phase-2 transition asphalt. Fair, not Good, because crack density >4/5m and surface oxidation indicates engineering distress emerging.

- 2016: Fair-leaning (utility patches, low longitudinal, no alligator)
- 2025: Same aged AC, but with progression — branching cracks in left wheelpath + patch failure scar

NO treatment boundary to flag.

text:
Yaws fully overlap. Both investigators saw the same patches in all 4 cardinal directions. The temporal claim is grounded:

- **2016**: dense interconnected alligator/block-hybrid cracks, OPEN/unsealed at yaw 0 (toward crosswalk)
- **2025**: same area now shows crack-seal applied to multiple transverse + longitudinal cracks, plus one small patch. No overlay.

This fits the captain's "stable Fair corridor with crack-seal maintenance only" narrative — but WP19 had denser distress in 2016 (alligator/block hybrid), and the city has since applied crack-seal maintenance. Currently Fair: visible engineering distress, sealed cracks indicate intervention but no structural overlay. No alligator visible currently (sealed), no potholes, but the underlying network was substantial.

Per rubric: crack_seal_visible counts as Fair-tier maintenance signal; underlying distress was alligator-adjacent. Sealed cracks are by definition Good-tolerable, but the density + small patch + oxidized untreated AC matrix puts this in Fair. No step-change from corridor pattern.

text:
Good — overlap is fine. The 2025 investigator actually covered all four cardinal yaws despite emphasizing 180. Both years show oxidized AC with longitudinal cracking; 2025 shows progression to incipient alligator in right wheelpath — consistent with deterioration over 9 years, NOT a step-change in either direction. This is still corridor-Fair territory.

Per the rubric: "incipient/branching, interconnected" alligator (confidence 0.75) at one wheelpath is the boundary case. The "no Fair-hedge" rule says: when uncertain Fair vs Poor, choose Poor. But the investigator called it "low-medium severity, incipient" — not yet full alligator field with spalling. Captain's directive flags pts 0-17 as Fair untreated. This looks like the same Fair pattern continuing, possibly slightly worse than typical.

I'll grade Fair with explicit note on the incipient alligator as a watch-item — but lean closer to Poor side of confidence. Actually, re-reading: "branching/incipient alligator network in right wheelpath" + "medium-severity longitudinal cracks 10-15mm unsealed" + transverse + raveling = this matches the Fair-to-Poor boundary. 10-15mm cracks are within Fair range (6-15mm). Incipient alligator is not yet "interconnected branching across full wheelpath" disqualifier. I'll go Fair but flag the trend.

text:
Interesting — there's a discrepancy. 2016 says PCC concrete intersection, 2025 says oxidized AC. Either:
1. The intersection has both AC and PCC sections and they looked at different patches
2. There was a reconstruction (PCC → AC overlay between 2016-2025) — unlikely
3. They looked at different yaws/areas

The 2016 investigator used yaw 90 as primary; 2025 investigator's yaw 90 was blocked and they used yaw 0. Let me check witness overlap.
Yaws overlap at 0 and 180. Temporal comparison is well-grounded. Now grading.

input.rationale: STEP-CHANGE from corridor's stable-Fair pattern. Cross-witnessed at matching yaw=180: 2016 showed only longitudinal sealed cracks at lane joint + scattered historical patches on oxidized grey AC (Fair, matching corridor pattern). 2025 same patch reveals high-severity alligator cracking along right edge/wheelpath with interconnected sharp-angled small pieces and edge-stripe paint disrupted by underlying failure — structural fatigue progression over 9 years on untreated AC. Per rubric Critical Rule #2 (alligator across wheelpath → Poor minimum) and the no-Fair-hedge rule, this is Poor. No fresh overlay or treatment between epochs explains absence of intervention. Edge break severity medium reinforces structural failure, not surface-only distress.

text:
Yaws fully overlap. So both investigators saw the same patches but came to different conclusions. The 2016 report says "PCC concrete intersection" while 2025 says "oxidized AC with sealed cracks + patch". 

This could be a real intersection with mixed surfaces (concrete intersection apron + AC approaches). The 2016 investigator focused on the concrete panels at yaw 90; 2025 investigator focused on AC at yaw 0 (since 90 was blocked). 

Given the captain's directive flags step-changes, and the 2016 evidence shows concrete intersection while 2025 shows oxidized AC... this may simply be that the intersection has concrete crosswalks/apron panels which 2016 captured, AND AC pavement in the through-lanes which 2025 captured. Both can be true at the same site.

For grading: the **current condition (2025)** is what matters most for the public-works decision, and 2025 shows the same Fair pattern as the rest of the corridor — oxidized AC with crack-seal and a patch. The 2016 PCC observation is real but doesn't contradict; it just means the intersection area has PCC panels visible from certain angles.

This is consistent with corridor narrative — Fair, stable, untreated AC + maintenance.

input.rationale: 2025 (yaw 0, primary): oxidized grey AC with multiple sealed transverse cracks and one rectangular mid-lane patch past intersection. No alligator, potholes, or rutting. Consistent with corridor Fair pattern (pts 0-38). 2016 (yaw 90): showed PCC concrete intersection panels with sealed joints and no distress — this is the concrete intersection apron/crosswalk area, NOT a contradiction; the AC through-lanes are what 2025's yaw 0 captured. Mixed-surface intersection with AC approaches and PCC apron. Grading on the AC through-lane condition (the dispatcher-relevant surface): Fair — visible engineering distress (transverse cracks) addressed by crack-seal, single patch, oxidized binder. No step-change from corridor.

text:
Excellent — this is highly diagnostic. At 2x zoom I can clearly see:

1. **A branching/interconnected crack network** in the center of the frame — this IS alligator-style cracking, with multiple sharp-angled connecting cracks. Pieces look ~15-30cm. The pattern is in a wheelpath area.
2. **A longitudinal crack** running parallel to the edge stripe, extending far down the lane
3. Surface is oxidized grey — untreated AC, no crack-sealing visible
4. The cracking is in the SAME APPROACH area as 2025's finding — at the intersection approach near the stop bar/crosswalk

So the alligator cracking WAS already present in 2016 at this same patch. This is a critical temporal finding — the defect was pre-existing, not progressed 2016→2025. Let me post claims.

text:
Excellent — the contrast is now crystal clear:

**Back (yaw 180/176°, east toward pt 41):** Heavy distress confirmed — I see:
- Multiple longitudinal cracks in right wheelpath
- Branching/interconnected crack patterns forming alligator at center-right
- A rectangular **utility patch** (left-center, ~1.5m square, mid-grey, distinct boundary)
- Mottled/raveled surface texture overall
- This is consistent with the chronic Poor zone from pt 41

**Forward (yaw 0°, west toward pt 46):** Pavement is markedly cleaner — uniform mid-grey surface, no visible alligator, lane markings crisp, no obvious wheelpath cracking. Looks like Fair/Sat condition.

This is a transition zone! Pt 42 is where Poor (east) → Fair (west). Let me post claims.

text:
Excellent — both investigators returned with usable, well-corroborated reports. Both covered yaw=0 and yaw=90 (overlap confirmed). The story is consistent: pt 43 is Fair-baseline, NOT an extension of the Poor zone at pts 40-41.

Let me verify the cross-witness overlap and then grade.

Strong reports. 2025 covered yaw 180 (the relevant Poor-zone-east direction), 2016 only covered yaw 0 and 90. For the temporal claim "2016 had only precursor cracks vs 2025 has alligator at yaw 180", I need 2016 at yaw 180 to compare same patch.

All four cardinal yaws overlap between epochs. Temporal comparison is grounded. The 2016 investigator covered all four (the report just listed the most diagnostic two). 

**Synthesis:** 
- 2025: extensive alligator+block cracking, both wheelpaths, eastbound approach (medium severity, 5-15mm cracks)
- 2016: alligator already present at intersection patch (low-medium), transverse cracks westbound lane
- No treatments visible in either epoch → chronic, untreated, progressing

This is **Poor** — alligator cracking is a Critical Decision Rule disqualifier. Confirms captain's hypothesis that the Poor zone extends through to pt 44.

input.rationale: Alligator+block cracking confirmed across both wheelpaths in 2025 (yaw=180 eastbound approach), with 5-15mm crack widths and 0.3-1m polygon pieces — medium severity. 2016 baseline at same point shows alligator already initiated (yaw=90 intersection patch) plus transverse/longitudinal cracking at yaw=0. Yaw overlap across all four cardinals (0/90/180/270) — temporal comparison grounded. No treatments visible in either epoch. Pattern: chronic untreated distress that has progressed/widened over 9 years. Confirms captain's directive — Poor zone from pts 40-41 extends through pt 44. Critical decision rule: alligator across wheelpath = Poor minimum.

text:
Yaws overlap at all 4 cardinals. The cross-witness verdict says we're grounded — both years covered yaw 0 (west, toward pt 46) AND yaw 180 (east, back toward Poor zone). That's exactly what I need.

Let me synthesize:
- **East (yaw 180)**: 2016 had longitudinal precursor cracks + intact patch; 2025 has full medium alligator + longitudinal — progression, no treatment. Consistent with chronic deterioration of Poor zone (pts 40-41).
- **West (yaw 0)**: 2025 cleaner — uniform, crisp markings, no alligator. Fair/Sat zone resumes here.

Pt 42 is a **transition point**. The dominant condition AT this point: medium alligator visible east-facing in the right/center wheelpath, longitudinal wheelpaths, but the western view is markedly better. The point straddles the Poor→Fair boundary.

Per the no-Fair-hedge rule: medium alligator cracking confirmed across two vantages = **Poor**. The fact that conditions improve immediately west informs the captain's narrative but the AT-POINT distress is structural.

input.rationale: Pt 42 sits at the western edge of the Poor zone. East view (yaw 180, toward chronic-alligator pts 40-41) shows medium alligator + longitudinal wheelpath cracks + intact utility patch in 2025; same patch in 2016 (yaws overlap, gate-grounded) showed only longitudinal precursor cracks and an intact oval patch — distress progressed Fair→Poor over 9 years with no treatment. West view (yaw 0, toward pt 46) is markedly cleaner: uniform pavement, crisp markings, no alligator. Per no-hedge rule, medium alligator confirmed = Poor, but this is the LAST Poor point before condition improves westward.

input.narrative:
## West Sunset Boulevard — Corridor Synthesis (47 points, 2,319 m)

**Overall character.** A mature, untreated HMA arterial. The driving surface across the entire corridor is the same generation of oxidized grey asphalt concrete, present already in 2016 imagery and unchanged in 2025 — no overlay, slurry, microsurface, or mill-and-overlay event in the 9-year arc. Maintenance has been reactive only: targeted crack-seal beads in wheelpaths and small utility-cut patches concentrated at intersections and manhole collars. The corridor is squarely in late Phase-2 of the deterioration S-curve, a high-value preservation candidate that has been deferred past its optimal treatment window.

**Tier distribution along the centerline.** The eastern and central 1,950 m (pts 1–39) are stable **Fair** — wheelpath longitudinals 5–10 mm, scattered transverse cracks, light raveling, faded markings, no alligator and no rutting. Cross-witnessed 2016↔2025 evidence shows slow, predictable progression with crack-seal maintenance largely keeping the surface from tipping into Poor. **Two distress hotspots break the pattern.** The east-end terminus (**pt 0**) shows medium alligator in 2016 imagery (no 2025 capture available within radius) — Poor by Critical Rule 2. A more significant **west-end Poor cluster runs ~250 m from pt 40 through pt 45** (pts 40, 41, 42, 44, 45 = Poor; pt 43 a Fair island between intersections), characterized by medium alligator in wheelpaths and at signalized intersection approaches, edge breaks, and a complete absence of crack-seal maintenance. Pt 41 alligator was already chronic in 2016 — 9+ years untreated. Pt 46 (corridor west terminus) recovers to Fair. A single **Good** point at pt 4 reflects a tree-shadowed local minimum of distress, not a treatment boundary.

**Treatment history.** No corridor-wide preservation event in the observed window. The crack-seal+patch maintenance regime is active across roughly pts 7–38 (intermittent, not continuous) but conspicuously **absent from the west-end Poor cluster (pts 40–45)** — suggesting maintenance jurisdiction or scheduling boundary rather than condition-driven priority. Several manhole collars (notably pts 7, 22, 28, 43) show settled or radially-cracked patches that have been chronic since 2016.

**Findings for the engineer.** (1) **West-end Poor cluster (pts 40–42, 44–45, ~2000–2250 m)** is the dispatch priority — chronic untreated alligator at signalized intersection approaches, braking-load concentration, accelerating toward pothole emergence. Recommend mill-and-overlay; preservation window has closed. (2) **Pt 0 east terminus** Poor — flag for verification, only 2016 imagery available, current state unknown. (3) **Pt 18 watch-item** — incipient alligator forming in right wheelpath in 2025 (absent in 2016) with no crack-seal applied; trending toward Poor. (4) **The remaining ~38 Fair points are a single corridor-scale microsurfacing/thin-overlay candidate** — treating now (PCI ~55–65) preserves $4–7/$1 cost ratio vs deferring to mill-and-overlay. (5) **Manhole collar repairs** at pts 7, 22, 28, 43 are independent of pavement scope but chronic and worth bundling. No `unknown` grades; cross-witnessing was clean across the corridor.
